---
title: "ARC Algorithm Pseudocode Reference"
subtitle: "Adaptive Regularization using Cubics"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    header-includes:
      - \usepackage{algorithm}
      - \usepackage{algpseudocode}
      - \usepackage{amsmath}
      - \usepackage{amssymb}
---

# Introduction

This document provides implementation-ready pseudocode for the arcopt package, which implements Adaptive Regularization using Cubics (ARC). A programmer unfamiliar with the project but knowledgeable in optimization should be able to implement the complete system from these specifications.

## Architecture Overview

The optimizer consists of **8 algorithm groups** organized into 4 layers:

| Layer | Components | Purpose |
|-------|------------|---------|
| **Orchestration** | Algorithm 1 (Main Loop) | Controls iteration flow, dispatches to sub-algorithms |
| **Core Solvers** | Algorithms 1a, 5 | Newton step, cubic subproblem solver |
| **Adaptation** | Algorithms 0, 2a/2b, 3 | Convergence, σ updates, momentum |
| **Safeguards** | Algorithms 6a/6b, 7, 8 | Robustness, constraints |

## System Flowchart

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 8
%%| fig-height: 10
flowchart TD
    subgraph INIT["Initialization"]
        A[x₀, σ₀, parameters] --> A2{fnscale ≠ 1?}
        A2 -->|Yes| A3[Wrap f, g, H<br/>with fnscale]
        A2 -->|No| B
        A3 --> B
        B{Constraints?}
        B -->|Box| C[Validate & project x₀<br/>Algorithm 7b]
        B -->|Linear Eq| D[Compute null space Z<br/>Transform to z-space<br/>Algorithm 8a-b]
        B -->|None| E[Use x₀ directly]
        C --> F[Main Loop]
        D --> F
        E --> F
    end

    subgraph MAIN["Main Loop (Algorithm 1)"]
        F --> G{Previous step<br/>rejected?}
        G -->|Yes| H[Skip evaluation<br/>Reuse f, g, H]
        G -->|No| I[Evaluate f, g, H]

        I --> J[Convergence Check<br/>Algorithm 0]
        H --> K[Cubic Subproblem]
        J -->|Converged| DONE[Return x*]
        J -->|Continue| L{Quasi-Newton?}

        L -->|Yes| K
        L -->|No| M[Try Newton Step<br/>Algorithm 1a]

        M -->|Success| N[Accept Newton step<br/>Decrease σ]
        M -->|Fail| K
    end

    subgraph CUBIC["Cubic Subproblem (Algorithm 5a/5b)"]
        K --> P[Eigendecomposition (5a)<br/>or ARCqK (5b)]
        P --> R[Step s]
    end

    subgraph EVAL["Step Evaluation"]
        R --> S{Box<br/>constraints?}
        S -->|Yes| T[Truncate step<br/>Algorithm 7a]
        S -->|No| U[Use s directly]
        T --> V[Evaluate f_trial]
        U --> V
        V --> W[Compute ρ = actual/predicted]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE["Updates"]
        W --> X{ρ ≥ η₁?}
        X -->|Yes| Y[Accept: x ← x + s]
        X -->|No| Z[Reject: x unchanged<br/>Mark rejected]

        Y --> AA[Update σ<br/>Algorithm 2a/2b]
        Z --> AA

        AA --> AB{Using<br/>momentum?}
        AB -->|Yes| AC[Apply momentum<br/>Algorithm 3]
        AB -->|No| AD[Continue]
        AC --> AD

        AD --> AE{Using SR1?}
        AE -->|Yes| AF[Update B<br/>Algorithm 4]
        AE -->|No| AG[Continue]
        AF --> AG

        AG --> AH{NaN/Inf<br/>detected?}
        AH -->|Yes| AI[Recovery<br/>Algorithm 6b]
        AH -->|No| AJ{Stagnation?}
        AI --> F
        AJ -->|Yes| AK[Recovery<br/>Algorithm 6a]
        AJ -->|No| F
        AK --> F
    end

    N --> AA

    style INIT fill:#e1f5fe
    style MAIN fill:#fff3e0
    style CUBIC fill:#f3e5f5
    style EVAL fill:#e8f5e9
    style UPDATE fill:#fce4ec
```

## Algorithm Index

| Algorithm | Name | Section | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------|---------|
| 0 | Convergence Check | §3 | Stan-style multi-criteria termination |
| 1 | Main ARC Loop | §4 | Orchestrates Newton-first + cubic + optional TR fallback dispatch |
| 1a | Newton Step | §4.2 | Gershgorin pre-check + Cholesky solve |
| 1c | TR-Mode Iteration | §4.3 | Full TR step/accept/radius-update cycle (active after Alg 6c fires) |
| 2a | CGT Update | §5.1 | Classical σ adaptation |
| 2b | Interpolation Update | §5.2 | Lipschitz-enhanced σ adaptation |
| 3 | ARCm | §6 | Momentum acceleration |
| 4 | Adaptive Inexact Cubic QN | §7 | QN with adaptive inexactness control |
| 4a | QN Hessian Updates | §7.1 | BFGS/SR1/L-BFGS/L-SR1 formulas |
| 4b | Accelerated Cubic QN | §7.2 | Nesterov-accelerated O(k⁻²) variant |
| 5a | Eigendecomposition Solver | §8.1 | Cubic subproblem via spectral decomposition (n ≤ 500) |
| 5b | Woodbury Solver | §8.2 | Low-rank QN cubic subproblem O(m²n) |
| 5c | TR Eigendecomposition Solver | §8.1a | Trust-region subproblem (activates after ridge detector fires) |
| 6a | Stagnation Recovery | §8.1 | Detect/recover from stagnation |
| 6b | NaN/Inf Protection | §8.2 | Handle numerical failures |
| 6c | Flat-Ridge Detector | §8.3 | Trigger cubic→TR switch on EB-violation proxy |
| 7a | Box Step Truncation | §9.1 | Apply bounds to step |
| 7b | Initial Point Projection | §9.1 | Validate starting point |
| 8a | Null Space Basis | §9.2 | Compute Z for linear equality |
| 8b | Space Transforms | §9.2 | Convert between x and z spaces |
| 8c | Constraint Projection | §9.2 | Project to feasible manifold |

## Computational Complexity

The table below shows per-call complexity. For the main loop (Algorithm 1), multiply by number of iterations K.

| Algorithm | Operations | f calls | g calls | H calls | Hv calls | Frequency | C++ Benefit |
|-----------|------------|---------|---------|---------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| 0 | O(n) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | Low (vectorized) |
| 1 | *orchestration* | 1-2 | 1 | 0-1 | 0 | — | Low |
| 1a | **O(n³)** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0-1/iter | **Medium** (Gershgorin loop) |
| 2a | O(1) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | None |
| 2b | O(1) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | None |
| 3 | O(n) | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | Low |
| 4 | O(n²) | 1-2 | 1-2 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | Low |
| 4a | O(n²) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | Low (rank-1/2 updates) |
| 4b | O(n²) | 1-2 | 1-2 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | Low |
| 5a | **O(n³)+O(n²·L)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0-1/iter | Low (LAPACK eigen) |
| 5b | O(m²n+m³·L) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0-1/iter | Medium (small matrix ops) |
| 6a | O(1) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | None |
| 6b | O(n³)×R | R | R | 0 | 0 | rare | Low |
| 7a | O(n) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | **Medium** (tight loop) |
| 7b | O(n) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | once | Low |
| 8a | O(n³) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | once | Low (LAPACK QR) |
| 8b | O(n·d) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2/iter | Low (BLAS) |
| 8c | O(m³) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | rare | Low (LAPACK) |

**Legend:**

- **L** = λ iterations in secular equation (typically 3-10)
- **J** = CG iterations in ARCqK (problem-dependent, typically 5-50)
- **m** = QN history size (typically 5-20) or number of shifts in ARCqK (typically 61)
- **R** = NaN retries (typically 0-3)
- **d** = n - rank(A), reduced dimension for linear equality constraints
- **f/g/H/Hv calls** = calls to user-provided objective, gradient, Hessian, or Hessian-vector product functions

**C++ Benefit Assessment:**

| Rating | Meaning | Examples |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **None** | O(1) operations, no loops | 2a, 2b, 6a |
| **Low** | Already uses BLAS/LAPACK or simple vectorized ops | 0, 3, 4, 5a, 8a-c |
| **Medium** | Tight loops over n elements, complex logic, or shift management | 1a (Gershgorin), 5b (CG+shifts), 7a (bounds) |
| **High** | Would require many R iterations | *None in current design* |

**Key Insights:**

1. **Algorithm 5a (Eigendecomposition)**: The expensive O(n³) eigendecomposition already calls LAPACK through R's `eigen()`. The secular iteration is O(n²·L) but L is small (3-10). **C++ benefit: Low**.

2. **Algorithm 5b (ARCqK)**: Each CG iteration involves:
   - One Hessian-vector product (user-provided, not our bottleneck)
   - O(m) scalar operations per shift (m = 61 typical)
   - Shift management logic (mark indefinite, check convergence, etc.)
   - Total: O(J·n·m) where J = CG iterations (5-50 typical)

   The shift management loop (m = 61 systems) with branching logic (indefinite detection, convergence checks) is not BLAS-able and could benefit from C++. **C++ benefit: Medium**.

3. **Where C++ helps most** (ranked by potential impact):
   - **Algorithm 5b (ARCqK shift loop)**: 61 shifts × J CG iterations with branching logic
   - **Algorithm 1a (Gershgorin)**: O(n²/2) tight loop over matrix elements
   - **Algorithm 7a (Box constraints)**: O(n) loop with conditionals

**Implementation Strategy:**

- **Phase 1 (Pure R)**: Implement both 5a and 5b in R. Use 5a (eigendecomposition) as default for n ≤ 500.
- **Phase 2 (Profile-guided)**: Profile on target problems. If 5b shift management dominates runtime, port to Rcpp.
- **Phase 3 (Optimization)**: Consider Rcpp for 1a and 7a only if profiling identifies them as bottlenecks.

## Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $x_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Current iterate |
| $f_k = f(x_k)$ | Function value |
| $g_k = \nabla f(x_k)$ | Gradient |
| $H_k = \nabla^2 f(x_k)$ | Hessian (or approximation $B_k$) |
| $\sigma_k > 0$ | Regularization parameter |
| $s_k$ | Step (search direction) |
| $\rho_k$ | Ratio of actual to predicted reduction |
| $\lambda$ | Cubic subproblem multiplier ($\lambda = \sigma\|s\|$) |
| $m_k(s)$ | Cubic model: $f_k + g_k^T s + \frac{1}{2} s^T H_k s + \frac{\sigma_k}{3}\|s\|^3$ |
| $B_k$ | Quasi-Newton Hessian approximation |
| $\delta_k$ | Inexactness tolerance (adaptive) |
| $\delta_x^y$ | Directional inexactness bound: $\|(H - B)(y-x)\| \le \delta_x^y \|y-x\|$ |
| $y_k = g_{k+1} - g_k$ | Gradient difference (secant equation) |
| $W, C$ | Low-rank factors: $B_m = B_0 + W^T C W$ |

## Execution Flow Summary

1. **Initialize**: Set $x_0$, $\sigma_0$; handle constraints if present
2. **Evaluate**: Compute $f_k$, $g_k$, $H_k$ (skip if previous step rejected)
3. **Check convergence**: 6 criteria (gradient, objective change, parameter change)
4. **Compute step**:
   - If analytic/FD Hessian: try Newton first (Gershgorin → Cholesky → Armijo)
   - If Newton fails or quasi-Newton: solve cubic subproblem
5. **Evaluate step**: Compute $\rho_k$; accept if $\rho_k \geq \eta_1$
6. **Update**: Adjust $\sigma_k$; apply momentum/SR1 if enabled; check safeguards
7. **Repeat** until converged

## Theoretical Guarantees

Unlike line search methods (which use Wolfe conditions), ARC convergence relies on the **ratio test** and **regularization bounds**. The following conditions must hold for the theoretical complexity guarantees of Cartis, Gould, and Toint (2011).

### Required Assumptions

| Assumption | Mathematical Statement | Practical Check |
|------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **A1**: Bounded below | $f(x) \geq f_{\text{low}}$ for all $x$ | Monitor $f_k$; warn if approaching machine limits |
| **A2**: Smooth objective | $f \in C^2$ (twice continuously differentiable) | User responsibility; check for NaN/Inf in $g$, $H$ |
| **A3**: Lipschitz Hessian | $\|\nabla^2 f(x) - \nabla^2 f(y)\| \leq L_H \|x - y\|$ | Cannot verify directly; assume holds |
| **A4**: Bounded $\sigma$ | $0 < \sigma_{\min} \leq \sigma_k \leq \sigma_{\max} < \infty$ | Enforced by Algorithm 2a/2b |
| **A5**: Subproblem accuracy | $s_k$ is an approximate global minimizer of $m_k(s)$ | Ensured by Algorithms 5a/5b |

### Guaranteed Complexity (Cartis-Gould-Toint 2011)

Under assumptions A1-A5:

| Target | Iterations to Achieve | Meaning |
|--------|----------------------|---------|
| $\|\nabla f(x_k)\| \leq \epsilon$ | $O(\epsilon^{-3/2})$ | First-order criticality |
| $\|\nabla f(x_k)\| \leq \epsilon$ and $\nabla^2 f(x_k) \succeq -\epsilon I$ | $O(\epsilon^{-3})$ | Second-order criticality |

This improves on trust-region/line-search methods which require $O(\epsilon^{-2})$ for first-order.

### Runtime Monitoring

The optimizer tracks these conditions at runtime and issues warnings when violations are detected. See **Algorithm 1, Step 9** for implementation details.

**Monitored conditions:**

- **Boundedness**: Warn if $f_k$ approaches machine limits
- **Smoothness**: Warn if NaN/Inf detected in derivatives
- **Ratio health**: Warn on repeated $\rho_k < 0$ (indicates model quality degradation)
- **Sigma saturation**: Warn if $\sigma_k = \sigma_{\max}$ but steps still rejected
- **Step quality**: Error if predicted reduction $\leq 0$ (subproblem solver failure)

### Comparison with Wolfe Conditions

| Aspect | Line Search (Wolfe) | ARC (Ratio Test) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Sufficient decrease** | $f(x_k + \alpha s) \leq f_k + c_1 \alpha g_k^T s$ | $\rho_k = \frac{f_k - f_{k+1}}{m_k(0) - m_k(s_k)} \geq \eta_1$ |
| **Curvature condition** | $g(x_k + \alpha s)^T s \geq c_2 g_k^T s$ | *Not needed*: cubic term handles curvature |
| **Step length** | Found by line search ($\alpha$) | Implicit in $\sigma$: larger $\sigma$ → shorter step |
| **Guarantees** | Global convergence | Global convergence + complexity bounds |
| **Parameters** | $c_1 \in (0, 1)$, $c_2 \in (c_1, 1)$ | $\eta_1 \in (0, 1)$, $\eta_2 \in (\eta_1, 1)$ |

**Key difference**: Wolfe conditions constrain the *step length* $\alpha$; ARC constrains the *model fidelity* via $\rho_k$. The regularization parameter $\sigma_k$ implicitly controls step size through the cubic term $\frac{\sigma}{3}\|s\|^3$.

# Diagnostics and Output

The optimizer provides two mechanisms for monitoring and debugging: **refresh** controls console output during optimization, and **trace** controls what diagnostic information is stored for post-hoc analysis.

## Refresh (Console Output)

Following Stan's convention, `refresh` controls how often iteration summaries are printed:

| `refresh` | Behavior |
|-----------|----------|
| 0 | Silent (no output) |
| 1 | Print every iteration |
| N | Print every N iterations |

**Output format:**

```
iter      f(x)        ||g||_inf      σ          step_type    time
   1   1.234e+02      5.67e-01   1.00e+00       newton      0.01s
   2   8.901e+01      3.21e-02   5.00e-01       cubic       0.02s
  10   2.345e-03      1.23e-06   1.25e-01       newton      0.01s
```

## Trace (Diagnostic Storage)

The `trace` parameter controls what information is stored per iteration for post-hoc analysis:

| Level | Stored | Memory |
|-------|--------|--------|
| 0 | Nothing (only final result) | O(n) |
| 1 | `f_k`, `||g_k||`, `converge_reason` | O(K) |
| 2 | + `σ_k`, `ρ_k`, `step_type`, `solver_used`, `rcond_k`, `iter_time` | O(K) |
| 3 | + `x_k`, `s_k`, `H_k`, all convergence criteria, `β_k` | O(Kn²) |

where K = number of iterations.

**Return structure** (when `trace ≥ 1`):

```r
result$trace <- list(
  # Level 1
  f = numeric(K),           # Function values
  g_norm = numeric(K),      # Gradient norms (infinity)
  converge_reason = character(K),  # Which criterion checked


  # Level 2 (additional)
  sigma = numeric(K),       # Regularization parameter
  rho = numeric(K),         # Actual/predicted reduction ratio
  step_type = character(K), # "newton", "cubic"
  solver_used = character(K), # "cholesky", "eigen" (future: "cg_lanczos")
  rcond = numeric(K),       # Reciprocal condition number of H_k
  lambda = numeric(K),      # Cubic subproblem multiplier (λ = σ||s||)
  time = numeric(K),        # Wall-clock seconds per iteration

  # Level 3 (additional)
  x = matrix(K, n),         # Iterates (K × n)
  s = matrix(K, n),         # Steps (K × n)
  H = array(K, n, n),       # Hessians (K × n × n)
  converge_criteria = list(...),  # All 6 convergence values per iteration
  beta = numeric(K)         # Momentum parameter (if using momentum)
)
```

**Default Parameters:**

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `refresh` | 10 | Print every 10 iterations (0 = silent) |
| `trace` | 1 | Store basic diagnostics |

# Convergence Checking

The optimizer uses multiple convergence criteria following Stan's L-BFGS implementation. Termination occurs when **any** criterion is satisfied. This multi-criteria approach prevents premature termination on ill-scaled problems while ensuring robust detection of true convergence.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 5 (Safeguarding) for discussion of convergence tolerances and stagnation detection.*

## Algorithm 0: Convergence Check (Stan-style)

```
ALGORITHM 0: Convergence Check
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  g_k             Current gradient
  f_k, f_{k-1}    Current and previous function values
  x_k, x_{k-1}    Current and previous iterates
  tol_grad        Absolute gradient tolerance
  tol_rel_grad    Relative gradient tolerance
  tol_obj         Absolute objective change tolerance
  tol_rel_obj     Relative objective change tolerance
  tol_param       Parameter change tolerance
  k               Iteration count
  max_iter        Maximum iterations

OUTPUT:
  converged       TRUE if any criterion satisfied
  reason          String describing which criterion triggered

PROCEDURE:
  1. CHECK ITERATION LIMIT:
     IF k ≥ max_iter THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "max_iter")
     END IF

  2. CHECK GRADIENT NORM (absolute):
     IF ||g_k||_∞ < tol_grad THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "gradient_abs")
     END IF

  3. CHECK GRADIENT NORM (relative):
     IF ||g_k||_∞ < tol_rel_grad × max(1, |f_k|) THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "gradient_rel")
     END IF

  4. CHECK OBJECTIVE CHANGE (absolute):
     IF k > 0 AND |f_k - f_{k-1}| < tol_obj THEN
       IF ||g_k||_∞ < 10 × tol_grad THEN    [gradient reasonably small]
         RETURN (TRUE, "objective_abs")
       END IF
     END IF

  5. CHECK OBJECTIVE CHANGE (relative):
     IF k > 0 AND |f_k - f_{k-1}| < tol_rel_obj × max(1, |f_k|) THEN
       IF ||g_k||_∞ < 10 × tol_grad THEN    [gradient reasonably small]
         RETURN (TRUE, "objective_rel")
       END IF
     END IF

  6. CHECK PARAMETER CHANGE:
     IF k > 0 AND ||x_k - x_{k-1}||_∞ < tol_param THEN
       IF ||g_k||_∞ < 10 × tol_grad THEN    [gradient reasonably small]
         RETURN (TRUE, "parameter")
       END IF
     END IF

  RETURN (FALSE, "")
```

**Implementation Notes:**

- Criteria 4-6 (objective/parameter changes) include a gradient safeguard (`||g_k||_∞ < 10 × tol_grad`) to prevent false convergence on non-stationary points. Small objective/parameter changes alone do not guarantee convergence; the gradient must also be reasonably small.
- Criteria 1-3 (iteration limit and gradient norms) are primary convergence indicators and do not require additional safeguards.

**Default Parameters** (following Stan):

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `tol_grad` | $10^{-8}$ | Absolute gradient tolerance |
| `tol_rel_grad` | $10^{7}$ | Relative gradient tolerance |
| `tol_obj` | $10^{-12}$ | Absolute objective change tolerance |
| `tol_rel_obj` | $10^{4}$ | Relative objective change tolerance |
| `tol_param` | $10^{-8}$ | Parameter change tolerance |
| `max_iter` | 1000 | Maximum iterations |

# Core ARC Algorithm

The main optimization loop attempts a Newton step first when conditions are favorable, falling back to cubic regularization when needed. This hybrid approach combines Newton's fast local convergence with ARC's robustness to indefinite Hessians.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 1 (Introduction) for motivation of the cubic model, and Section 2 for regularization parameter adaptation strategies.*

## Algorithm 1: ARC with Newton-First Strategy (and optional cubic→TR fallback)

```
ALGORITHM 1: ARC with Newton-First Strategy
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_0           Initial point
  σ_0           Initial regularization parameter
  η_1, η_2      Acceptance thresholds
  γ             Multiplicative update factor
  σ_min, σ_max  Regularization bounds
  c_armijo      Armijo sufficient decrease parameter (e.g., 1e-4)
  fnscale       Objective scaling (1 = minimize, -1 = maximize)
  tol_lambda    Cubic solver λ tolerance (e.g., 1e-10)
  max_iter_lambda  Cubic solver max iterations (e.g., 100)
  refresh       Print frequency (0 = silent, N = every N iterations)
  trace         Diagnostic level (0-3)
  [Trust-region fallback controls — Algorithm 6c + 1c + 5c:]
  tr_fallback_enabled     Enable cubic→TR one-way switch (default TRUE)
  tr_fallback_window W    Detector window length (default 10)
  tr_fallback_tol_ridge   λ_min threshold for "ill-conditioned" (default 1e-3)
  tr_fallback_rho_tol     |ρ − 1| tolerance (default 0.1)
  tr_fallback_grad_decrease_max  Gradient-stagnation ratio (default 0.9)
  tr_fallback_g_inf_floor Absolute ||g||_∞ floor (default 1e-6)
  tr_r0, tr_rmax          Initial / maximum TR radius (default 1, 100)
  tr_eta1, tr_eta2        TR acceptance / expansion thresholds
  tr_gamma_shrink, tr_gamma_grow   TR radius update factors

OUTPUT:
  x*                Approximate minimizer (or maximizer if fnscale = -1)
  f*                Function value at x* (original scale)
  g*                Gradient at x* (original scale)
  H*                Hessian at x* (original scale)
  converge_info     Convergence status and reason
  solver_mode_final Which subproblem solver was active at termination
                    ∈ {"cubic", "tr"}
  ridge_switches    Number of cubic→TR transitions (0 or 1 in v1)
  radius_final      Final TR radius (NA if solver_mode_final == "cubic")
  trace_data        Diagnostic history (if trace ≥ 1)

PROCEDURE:
  [WRAPPER: Apply fnscale for maximization support]
  IF fnscale ≠ 1 THEN
    f_orig ← f
    g_orig ← g
    H_orig ← H
    f ← λx. fnscale × f_orig(x)
    g ← λx. fnscale × g_orig(x)
    H ← λx. fnscale × H_orig(x)
  END IF

  k ← 0
  prev_rejected ← FALSE              [track if previous step was rejected]
  prev_used_newton ← FALSE           [track if previous iteration used Newton]
  consecutive_negative_rho ← 0       [for monitoring theoretical guarantees]

  [Trust-region fallback state — inactive by default, activates on
   flat-ridge or stuck-indefinite-saddle detection; see Algorithm 6c]
  solver_mode ← "cubic"
  ridge_state ← init_flat_ridge_state(W = tr_fallback_window)
  radius_current ← NA                [becomes meaningful once solver_mode = "tr"]
  ridge_switches ← 0

  IF trace ≥ 1 THEN initialize_trace_storage() END IF

  WHILE NOT converged DO
    iter_start_time ← clock()

    1. EVALUATE (skip if previous step was rejected):
       IF NOT prev_rejected THEN
         f_k ← f(x_k)
         g_k ← ∇f(x_k)
         H_k ← get_hessian(x_k, hess_method)    [or B_k if quasi-Newton]
       END IF
       [If prev_rejected: f_k, g_k, H_k unchanged from previous iteration]

    2. CHECK CONVERGENCE:
       (converged, reason) ← convergence_check(...)    [Algorithm 0]
       IF converged THEN BREAK END IF

    2b. CHECK STAGNATION:
       action ← detect_stagnation(step_norms, f_values, ...)    [Algorithm 6a]
       IF action = "stop" THEN
         [Stagnation detected - verify gradient before stopping]
         IF ||g_k||_∞ < gtol_abs THEN
           converged ← TRUE
           reason ← "stagnation"
           BREAK
         ELSE
           [Stagnation but gradient not small - unusual situation]
           already_refreshed ← TRUE    [prevent infinite stagnation loops]
         END IF
       ELSE IF action = "refresh_hessian" THEN
         [For quasi-Newton: refresh Hessian approximation]
         already_refreshed ← TRUE
       END IF

    3. SOLVER-MODE DISPATCH:
       [Once the flat-ridge detector has fired (solver_mode = "tr"),
        skip the Newton + cubic pipeline entirely and run a
        trust-region iteration instead. The TR iteration is a full
        step-compute/accept-reject/radius-update cycle; see
        Algorithm 1c for detail.]
       IF solver_mode = "tr" THEN
         (s_k, ρ_k, accepted, r_new, on_boundary) ←
           tr_mode_iteration(x_k, f_k, g_k, H_k, r_k = radius_current,
                             control)    [Algorithm 1c]
         step_type ← "tr"
         IF accepted THEN
           x_{k+1} ← x_k + s_k
           [f_{k+1}, g_{k+1}, H_{k+1} are produced by Algorithm 1c]
           prev_rejected ← FALSE
         ELSE
           x_{k+1} ← x_k
           prev_rejected ← TRUE
         END IF
         radius_current ← r_new
         [NOTE: σ is NOT updated in TR mode; r_k replaces its role.
          The detector is also not re-evaluated in TR mode in v1 — the
          switch is one-way.]
         GOTO STEP 9    [proceed to diagnostics]
       END IF

    [solver_mode == "cubic": execute the Newton-first + cubic pipeline,
     then update the flat-ridge detector at the end]

    3a. ATTEMPT NEWTON STEP (skip if previously rejected):
       step_type ← "cubic"    [default]
       IF NOT prev_rejected THEN
         (newton_success, s_newton, pred_quad) ← try_newton_step(g_k, H_k)

         [Sigma reset safeguard: If previous Newton succeeded but current fails,
          Hessian likely changed from PD to indefinite. Reset sigma to provide
          adequate regularization for cubic solver in new region.]
         IF NOT newton_success AND prev_used_newton THEN
           σ_k ← σ_0    [reset to initial value]
         END IF

         IF newton_success THEN
           [Evaluate Newton trial point]
           x_trial ← x_k + s_newton
           f_trial ← f(x_trial)

           [Check for NaN/Inf in trial evaluation]
           IF NOT is_finite(f_trial) THEN
             newton_success ← FALSE    [treat as failure, will use cubic]
           END IF
         END IF

         IF newton_success THEN
           [Compute ratio using quadratic model]
           actual_reduction ← f_k - f_trial
           ρ_k ← actual_reduction / pred_quad

           [Accept or reject based on ratio test]
           IF is_finite(ρ_k) AND ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
             [Accept Newton step]
             s_k ← s_newton
             x_{k+1} ← x_trial
             f_{k+1} ← f_trial
             prev_rejected ← FALSE
             step_type ← "newton"

             [Update sigma using standard algorithm]
             σ_{k+1} ← update_sigma(σ_k, ρ_k, η_1, η_2, γ, σ_min, σ_max)
             [Algorithm 2a or 2b - same as cubic steps]

             GOTO STEP 9    [skip cubic solver, proceed to gradient/Hessian eval]
           ELSE
             [Reject Newton step - will fall through to cubic solver]
             prev_rejected ← TRUE
           END IF
         END IF
       END IF
       [If Newton skipped/failed/rejected: proceed to cubic solver]

    4. CUBIC SUBPROBLEM (Newton failed or rejected):
       (s_k, λ_k) ← solve_cubic_subproblem(g_k, H_k, σ_k, cubic_solver)
       [Algorithm 5a (eigendecomposition) based on control$cubic_solver]

    5. EVALUATE TRIAL POINT:
       x_trial ← x_k + s_k
       f_trial ← f(x_trial)

    6. COMPUTE RATIO:
       pred_reduction ← m_k(0) - m_k(s_k)
       IF pred_reduction ≤ 0 THEN
         ERROR("Cubic subproblem solver failed: predicted reduction non-positive")
       END IF
       actual_reduction ← f_k - f_trial
       ρ_k ← actual_reduction / pred_reduction

    7. UPDATE ITERATE AND TRACK REJECTION:
       IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
         x_{k+1} ← x_trial    [accept step]
         prev_rejected ← FALSE
       ELSE
         x_{k+1} ← x_k        [reject step]
         prev_rejected ← TRUE
         [Next iteration: skip eval, skip Newton, re-solve cubic with larger σ]
       END IF

    8. UPDATE REGULARIZATION:
       σ_{k+1} ← update_sigma(σ_k, ρ_k, η_1, η_2, γ, σ_min, σ_max)
       [Algorithm 2a or 2b]

    8b. UPDATE FLAT-RIDGE DETECTOR (cubic mode only):
       [Observe the current iteration's runtime signals; fire a one-way
        switch to TR mode if all four signals have held for W
        consecutive iterations. See Algorithm 6c for signal details.]
       IF tr_fallback_enabled AND is_finite(ρ_k) THEN
         λ_min_k ← min_eigenvalue(H_k)
         ridge_state ← update_flat_ridge_state(
                         ridge_state,
                         σ = σ_{k+1}, ρ = ρ_k,
                         g_inf = ||g_k||_∞, λ_min = λ_min_k)
         IF check_flat_ridge_trigger(ridge_state, σ_min, tol_ridge,
                                     rho_tol, grad_decrease_max,
                                     g_inf_floor) THEN
           solver_mode ← "tr"
           radius_current ← min(tr_r0, tr_rmax)
           ridge_switches ← ridge_switches + 1
           [NOTE: B_0 for TR is the current H_k; no extra hess() eval
            at the switch. σ_{k+1} is retained in state but unused
            once solver_mode = "tr".]
         END IF
       END IF

    9. DIAGNOSTICS AND MONITORING:
       iter_time ← clock() - iter_start_time

       [MONITORING: Check theoretical guarantee conditions]
       IF f_k < f_low_warn THEN
         WARN("Objective approaching lower bound limit")
       END IF

       IF ρ_k < 0 THEN
         consecutive_negative_rho ← consecutive_negative_rho + 1
         IF consecutive_negative_rho > 3 THEN
           WARN("Repeated ρ < 0: model quality degraded, L_H assumption may be violated")
         END IF
       ELSE
         consecutive_negative_rho ← 0
       END IF

       IF σ_k = σ_max AND ρ_k < η_1 THEN
         WARN("σ saturated at maximum but steps still rejected")
       END IF

       [TRACE: Store iteration data]
       IF trace ≥ 1 THEN
         store_trace_level1(k, f_k, ||g_k||_∞, reason)
       END IF
       IF trace ≥ 2 THEN
         store_trace_level2(k, σ_k, ρ_k, step_type, iter_time)
       END IF
       IF trace ≥ 3 THEN
         store_trace_level3(k, x_k, s_k, H_k, converge_criteria, ...)
       END IF

       [REFRESH: Print progress]
       IF refresh > 0 AND k MOD refresh = 0 THEN
         PRINT iter=k, f=f_k, ||g||=||g_k||_∞, σ=σ_k, step=step_type, time=iter_time
       END IF

       [Track if current iteration used Newton for next iteration's sigma reset]
       prev_used_newton ← (step_type = "newton")

    k ← k + 1
  END WHILE

  [Return original-scale values]
  IF fnscale ≠ 1 THEN
    f_k ← f_k / fnscale    [convert back to original scale]
    g_k ← g_k / fnscale
    H_k ← H_k / fnscale
  END IF

  RETURN x_k, f_k, g_k, H_k, converge_info
```

## Algorithm 1a: Newton Step

This subroutine attempts a Newton step using Cholesky factorization to check positive definiteness and solve the Newton system.

**No pre-filtering by σ is required**: The literature shows that Newton steps should be attempted whenever the Hessian appears positive definite, with the ratio test ρₖ determining acceptance. See `literature/consensus_reviews/No explicit σ-threshold for Newton Step.txt` for details.

```
ALGORITHM 1a: Try Newton Step
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  g_k         Gradient
  H_k         Hessian (analytic or FD, NOT quasi-Newton)

OUTPUT:
  success     TRUE if Newton step computable (H_k is PD)
  s_newton    Newton step (if successful)
  pred_quad   Predicted reduction from quadratic model

PROCEDURE:
  1. ATTEMPT CHOLESKY:
     (L, chol_success) ← cholesky(H_k)
     IF NOT chol_success THEN
       RETURN (FALSE, NULL, NULL)    [H_k is indefinite]
     END IF

  2. SOLVE NEWTON SYSTEM:
     s_newton ← -L^{-T} L^{-1} g_k    [i.e., s = -H_k^{-1} g_k]

  3. COMPUTE PREDICTED REDUCTION (quadratic model):
     [Newton minimizes m_quad(s) = f + g^T s + (1/2) s^T H s]
     pred_quad ← -(g_k^T s_newton + (1/2) s_newton^T H_k s_newton)

     [Safeguard: ensure non-negative predicted reduction]
     IF pred_quad < 0 THEN
       RETURN (FALSE, NULL, NULL)    [descent direction violated]
     END IF

  RETURN (TRUE, s_newton, pred_quad)
```

**Why Newton-First?**

- Newton converges quadratically near solutions; ARC adds overhead when Newton works
- Gershgorin check is O(n²) vs Cholesky's O(n³) — cheap to try first
- Only attempt Newton with analytic or FD Hessians for accurate curvature information

## Algorithm 1c: Trust-Region Mode Iteration

Executed by the main loop (Algorithm 1, step 3) when `solver_mode = "tr"` —
i.e., after the flat-ridge detector (Algorithm 6c) has fired and performed
the one-way switch from cubic regularization to trust-region. Replaces the
Newton-attempt (Algorithm 1a) + cubic-subproblem (Algorithm 5a) pipeline
with a single trust-region subproblem solve (Algorithm 5c) followed by a
step-acceptance test and radius update.

```
ALGORITHM 1c: Trust-Region Mode Iteration
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_k                  Current iterate
  f_k                  Current function value
  g_k                  Current gradient
  H_k                  Current Hessian
  r_k                  Current trust-region radius (> 0)
  tr_eta1              Acceptance threshold (default 0.25)
  tr_eta2              Expansion threshold (default 0.75)
  tr_gamma_shrink      Radius shrink factor (default 0.25)
  tr_gamma_grow        Radius grow factor (default 2.0)
  tr_rmax              Maximum radius (default 100)
  fn, gr, hess         Objective callbacks
  lower, upper         Box bounds (optional)

OUTPUT:
  s_k          TR step (may be rejected)
  ρ_k          Ratio of actual to predicted reduction
  accepted     TRUE if step was accepted
  r_{k+1}      Updated radius
  on_boundary  TRUE if ‖s_k‖ == r_k (active constraint)

PROCEDURE:
  1. SOLVE TR SUBPROBLEM (Algorithm 5c):
     (s_k, λ_k, pred_reduction, converged, case_type, on_boundary) ←
       solve_tr_eigen(g_k, H_k, radius = r_k,
                      tol_lambda, max_iter_lambda, hard_case_tol)

  2. APPLY BOX CONSTRAINTS (same as cubic path):
     (s_k, x_trial) ← apply_box_constraints(x_k, s_k, lower, upper)
     [Algorithm 7a]

  3. EVALUATE TRIAL POINT:
     f_trial ← f(x_trial)
     fn_evals ← fn_evals + 1

     IF NOT is_finite(f_trial) THEN
       [Non-finite trial — shrink radius and skip this iteration]
       r_{k+1} ← max(r_k · tr_gamma_shrink, machine_epsilon)
       RETURN (s_k, ρ_k = -∞, accepted = FALSE, r_{k+1}, on_boundary)
     END IF

  4. COMPUTE RATIO:
     actual_reduction ← f_k − f_trial
     IF |pred_reduction| < machine_epsilon THEN
       [Guard against degenerate model]
       ρ_k ← 1.0 IF actual_reduction ≥ 0 ELSE 0.0
     ELSE
       ρ_k ← actual_reduction / pred_reduction
     END IF

  5. ACCEPT OR REJECT:
     IF is_finite(ρ_k) AND ρ_k ≥ tr_eta1 THEN
       [Accept: evaluate new gradient and Hessian]
       x_{k+1} ← x_trial
       f_{k+1} ← f_trial
       g_{k+1} ← ∇f(x_{k+1})
       H_{k+1} ← ∇²f(x_{k+1})
       gr_evals ← gr_evals + 1
       hess_evals ← hess_evals + 1
       accepted ← TRUE
     ELSE
       [Reject: keep current iterate, recycle gradient/Hessian]
       x_{k+1} ← x_k
       f_{k+1} ← f_k
       g_{k+1} ← g_k
       H_{k+1} ← H_k
       accepted ← FALSE
     END IF

  6. UPDATE RADIUS:
     IF NOT is_finite(ρ_k) OR ρ_k < tr_eta1 THEN
       [Poor step: shrink]
       r_{k+1} ← max(r_k · tr_gamma_shrink, machine_epsilon)
     ELSE IF ρ_k ≥ tr_eta2 AND on_boundary THEN
       [Very good step hitting the boundary: grow radius]
       r_{k+1} ← min(r_k · tr_gamma_grow, tr_rmax)
     ELSE
       [Acceptable step in interior, or very good step not at boundary:
        keep radius]
       r_{k+1} ← r_k
     END IF

  RETURN (s_k, ρ_k, accepted, r_{k+1}, on_boundary)
```

### Comparison with cubic-mode iteration (Algorithm 1, steps 3a–8)

Both modes share the same outer scaffolding (evaluate, check convergence
and stagnation, box-constrain step, evaluate trial, accept by ρ test,
diagnostics). The differences are localized:

| Step | Cubic mode | TR mode |
|------|-----------|---------|
| Subproblem | Newton (Alg 1a) → cubic (Alg 5a) | Trust-region (Alg 5c) |
| Regularization knob | σ_k via Alg 2a/2b | r_k via shrink/grow |
| Predicted reduction | −g^T s − ½ s^T H s − (σ/3)‖s‖³ | −g^T s − ½ s^T H s |
| Accept threshold | η_1 (default 0.1) | tr_eta1 (default 0.25) |
| Expand condition | η_2 for σ reduction | tr_eta2 AND on_boundary for r growth |
| Detector update | YES (Alg 6c can fire switch to TR) | NO (one-way switch in v1) |

The mode dispatch in Algorithm 1 step 3 branches between these two
pipelines based on the `solver_mode` state. On a run that never fires
the detector, TR mode is never entered and the behavior is identical to
the pre-hybrid algorithm.

**Implementation status**: Fully implemented in the main loops of
`arcopt()` and `arcopt_qn()` on branch `feature/tr-fallback-hybrid`
(commit `a3fed60`). In `arcopt_qn()`, H_k is replaced by the QN
approximation B_k (which is still maintained by SR1/BFGS/hybrid updates
after each accepted TR step).

---

# Regularization Parameter Updates

The regularization parameter $\sigma_k$ controls the trade-off between the quadratic model and the cubic safeguard. These algorithms adapt $\sigma_k$ based on how well the model predicted actual function decrease.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 2 for detailed comparison of CGT (Option 1) and Interpolation-Enhanced (Option 2) adaptation strategies.*

## Algorithm 2a: Classical CGT Update

The standard parameter update from Cartis, Gould, and Toint (2011). Simple, robust, and theoretically well-understood.

```
ALGORITHM 2a: Classical CGT Update
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  σ_k         Current regularization parameter
  ρ_k         Ratio of actual to predicted reduction
  η_1, η_2    Thresholds (0 < η_1 < η_2 < 1)
  γ           Multiplicative factor (γ > 1)
  σ_min       Minimum allowed σ
  σ_max       Maximum allowed σ

OUTPUT:
  σ_{k+1}     Updated regularization parameter

PROCEDURE:
  IF ρ_k ≥ η_2 THEN
    σ_{k+1} ← max(σ_k / γ, σ_min)     [very successful]
  ELSE IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
    σ_{k+1} ← σ_k                      [successful]
  ELSE
    σ_{k+1} ← min(γ · σ_k, σ_max)     [unsuccessful]
  END IF

  RETURN σ_{k+1}
```

## Algorithm 2b: Interpolation-Enhanced CGT Update

Variant that estimates local Lipschitz constant from model-function discrepancy (Gould, Porcelli, Toint, 2012). May provide faster adaptation on large-scale problems with varying curvature.

```
ALGORITHM 2b: Interpolation-Enhanced CGT Update
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  σ_k         Current regularization parameter
  ρ_k         Ratio of actual to predicted reduction
  s_k         Current step
  f(x_k+s_k)  Function value at trial point
  m_k(s_k)    Model value at step
  η_1, η_2    Thresholds
  γ           Multiplicative factor
  σ_min       Minimum allowed σ
  σ_max       Maximum allowed σ

OUTPUT:
  σ_{k+1}     Updated regularization parameter

PROCEDURE:
  1. ESTIMATE LOCAL LIPSCHITZ CONSTANT:
     L̂_k ← 2 |f(x_k + s_k) - m_k(s_k)| / ||s_k||³

  2. UPDATE WITH INTERPOLATION:
     IF ρ_k ≥ η_2 THEN
       σ_{k+1} ← max(L̂_k / 2, σ_k / γ, σ_min)     [very successful]
     ELSE IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
       σ_{k+1} ← σ_k                               [successful]
     ELSE
       σ_{k+1} ← max(L̂_k / 2, γ · σ_k)            [unsuccessful]
       σ_{k+1} ← min(σ_{k+1}, σ_max)
     END IF

  RETURN σ_{k+1}
```

**Default Parameters**:

| Parameter | Default | Valid Range | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `sigma_init` | 1.0 | $(0, \infty)$ | Initial $\sigma_0$ |
| `eta_1` | 0.1 | $(0, \eta_2)$ | Acceptance threshold |
| `eta_2` | 0.9 | $(\eta_1, 1)$ | Very successful threshold |
| `gamma` | 2.0 | $(1, \infty)$ | Multiplicative factor |
| `sigma_min` | $10^{-6}$ | $(0, \sigma_0)$ | Lower bound (may need $10^{-2}$ for indefinite problems) |
| `sigma_max` | $10^{12}$ | $(\sigma_0, \infty)$ | Upper bound |

# Momentum Acceleration

Momentum acceleration is **disabled by default** (`use_momentum = FALSE`) due to potential oscillation with small momentum coefficients (momentum_c1, momentum_c2). When enabled with aggressive parameters (momentum_c1 = 1.0, momentum_c2 = 1.0), it can reduce iterations on some ill-conditioned problems. However, conservative default values (c1 = 0.1, c2 = 0.1) may cause convergence failure or slow progress on functions like Dixon-Price. Users should enable momentum only after testing on their specific problem class.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 4 for theoretical background on momentum with cubic regularization (CRm, ARCm variants) and complexity analysis.*

## Algorithm 3: ARCm (Momentum-Accelerated ARC)

```
ALGORITHM 3: ARCm (Momentum-Accelerated ARC) - Gao et al. 2022
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_0           Initial point
  σ_0           Initial regularization
  τ             Maximum momentum parameter (default: 0.5)
  α_1, α_2      Momentum scaling constants (default: 0.1, 1.0)
  [Standard ARC parameters: η_1, η_2, γ_1, γ_2, σ_min, σ_max]

OUTPUT:
  x*            Approximate minimizer

PROCEDURE:
  k ← 0
  v_{-1} ← 0    [initialize momentum vector]

  WHILE NOT converged DO    [see Algorithm 0]

    1. EVALUATE at x_k:
       f_k ← f(x_k), g_k ← ∇f(x_k), H_k ← ∇²f(x_k)

    2. SOLVE CUBIC SUBPROBLEM:
       s_k ← argmin_s [g_k^T s + (1/2)s^T H_k s + (σ_k/3)||s||³]

    3. SET TRIAL POINT:
       y_{k+1} ← x_k + s_k
       f(y_{k+1}) ← f(y_{k+1})

    4. COMPUTE ρ_k:
       [Standard ratio test - see Algorithm 2a]

    5. APPLY MOMENTUM (if step accepted):
       IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
         [Compute beta upper bound]
         β_upper ← min{τ, α_1 ||s_k||, α_2 ||s_k||²}

         [Bisection search for β_k satisfying f(z_{k+1}) ≤ f(y_{k+1})]
         β_k ← bisection_search([0, β_upper], f(y_{k+1}))

         [Update with momentum]
         v_k ← β_k · v_{k-1} + s_k
         x_{k+1} ← x_k + v_k
       ELSE
         [Rejected step: no update, momentum carries forward]
         x_{k+1} ← x_k
         v_k ← v_{k-1}
       END IF

    6. UPDATE σ_k:
       [Standard CGT update - see Algorithm 2a]

    k ← k + 1
  END WHILE

  RETURN x_k

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NOTES:

1. Momentum vector v_k accumulates: v_k = β_k · v_{k-1} + s_k
   - Far from optimum (||s|| large): β ≈ τ (maximum momentum)
   - Near optimum (||s|| → 0): β → 0 (preserves second-order convergence)

2. Bisection search ensures monotonicity: f(x_{k+1}) ≤ f(y_{k+1})
   - This prevents momentum from increasing function value
   - Typically converges in 5-10 bisection iterations

3. Rejected steps preserve v_k for next iteration
   - Allows momentum to help recover from over-regularization
```

**Momentum Parameters**:

| Parameter | Default | Valid Range | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `momentum_tau` | 0.5 | $(0, 1)$ | Maximum $\beta_k$ (Gao's $\tau$) |
| `momentum_alpha1` | 0.1 | $(0, \infty)$ | Linear step scaling (Gao's $\alpha_1$) |
| `momentum_alpha2` | 1.0 | $(0, \infty)$ | Quadratic step scaling (Gao's $\alpha_2$) |

# Quasi-Newton Acceleration

This section specifies the Accelerated Cubic Regularized Quasi-Newton framework based on Kamzolov et al. (2023, 2025). Unlike traditional quasi-Newton methods that achieve at best $O(\epsilon^{-2})$ global convergence, combining QN with cubic regularization enables $O(k^{-1})$ non-accelerated and $O(k^{-2})$ accelerated rates—matching gradient descent and accelerated gradient descent respectively.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd §sec-cubic-qn for theoretical background on directional inexactness and convergence analysis.*

## Key Innovation: Directional Inexactness

Classical inexact Hessian theory requires $\|H_k - B_k\|_2 \le \delta$ (accuracy in all directions). This is too stringent for QN approximations.

**Directional inexactness** (Kamzolov et al.) only requires accuracy along the step direction:

$$\|(H_k - B_k)(x_{k+1} - x_k)\| \le \delta_k^{x_{k+1}} \|x_{k+1} - x_k\|$$

This weaker condition is naturally satisfied by BFGS, SR1, and DFP updates, enabling global convergence guarantees for cubic QN methods.

## Architecture: Dual Path Design

The `arcopt()` function provides two distinct execution paths based on the `use_qn` control parameter:

```
arcopt(x0, fn, gr, hess = NULL, control)
  │
  ├─ use_qn = FALSE ──→ Standard ARC Path (Algorithm 1)
  │                      • Requires: exact Hessian or FD approximation
  │                      • Subproblem: Eigendecomposition (Algorithm 5a)
  │                      • Adaptation: σ only (Algorithms 2a/2b)
  │                      • Acceleration: Momentum (Algorithm 3)
  │
  └─ use_qn = TRUE ───→ QN-ARC Path (Algorithm 4)
                         • Hessian: QN approximation B_k (Algorithm 4a)
                         • Subproblem: Woodbury solver (Algorithm 5b)
                         • Adaptation: σ + δ (inexactness control)
                         • Acceleration: Nesterov extrapolation (Algorithm 4b)

Shared Components (both paths):
  • Convergence checking (Algorithm 0)
  • Constraint handling (Algorithms 7, 8)
  • Safeguards (Algorithms 6a, 6b)
  • Trace logging and diagnostics
```

**When to use which path:**

| Scenario | Recommended Path | Rationale |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| Analytic Hessian available, n ≤ 500 | Standard ARC | Exact curvature, proven robustness |
| Analytic Hessian available, n > 500 | QN-ARC (hybrid) | Use H for init/refresh, QN for speed |
| FD Hessian required, n ≤ 100 | Standard ARC | FD feasible for small problems |
| FD Hessian required, n > 100 | QN-ARC (pure) | Avoid O(n²) gradient calls |
| Ill-conditioned problems | Standard ARC | Exact eigenvalues handle indefiniteness |
| Well-conditioned, many variables | QN-ARC | Faster per-iteration with L-BFGS |

## Algorithm 4: Adaptive Inexact Cubic Regularized QN

This is the main outer method that uses QN Hessian approximations with adaptive inexactness control.

**Hybrid Design**: When an exact Hessian function is provided, it is used for:
1. **Initialization**: B₀ = H(x₀) instead of scaled identity
2. **Periodic refresh**: Recompute B_k = H(x_k) when approximation degrades

This enables a cost-benefit tradeoff: occasional expensive Hessian evaluations prevent many wasted iterations with poor approximations.

```
ALGORITHM 4: Adaptive Inexact Cubic Regularized QN (Kamzolov et al. 2025)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_0           Initial point
  hess          Exact Hessian function H(x) [optional - enables hybrid mode]
  σ_0           Initial regularization parameter
  δ_0           Initial inexactness tolerance
  γ_inc         Inexactness increase factor (default: 2.0)
  γ_dec         Inexactness decrease factor (default: 0.5)
  δ_max         Maximum tolerance before refresh (default: 10.0)
  η_1, η_2      Acceptance thresholds (same as Algorithm 2a)
  γ             Regularization update factor (same as Algorithm 2a)
  M             Upper bound on Hessian Lipschitz constant (or 2L_2)
  qn_method     "bfgs", "sr1", "lbfgs", or "lsr1"
  qn_memory     Memory parameter m for limited-memory variants
  refresh_period  Iterations between forced refresh (0 = disabled)

OUTPUT:
  x*            Approximate minimizer
  B*            Final Hessian approximation

PROCEDURE:
  k ← 0
  consecutive_skips ← 0    [SR1 skip counter]

  (Step 0) INITIALIZE HESSIAN APPROXIMATION:
    IF hess is provided THEN
      B_0 ← hess(x_0)              [Exact Hessian initialization]
      refresh_available ← TRUE
    ELSE
      [FD Hessian from gradient - the default as of v0.1.1.
       Costs 2*n gradient evaluations once at startup but
       injects negative-curvature information into B_0, which
       identity initialization would miss.]
      B_0 ← fd_hessian_from_grad(∇f, x_0)
      refresh_available ← TRUE     [FD refresh is always available]
    END IF

  WHILE NOT converged DO    [see Algorithm 0]

    1. EVALUATE:
       f_k ← f(x_k)
       g_k ← ∇f(x_k)
       [B_k already available from previous iteration or initialization]

    2. SOLVE INEXACT CUBIC SUBPROBLEM:
       [Minimize cubic model with QN Hessian and inexactness regularization]
       s_k ← argmin_s { g_k^T s + ½ s^T B_k s + (M/6)||s||³ + (δ_k/2)||s||² }

       [Use Algorithm 5b (Woodbury solver) for low-rank B_k]
       x_{k+1}^{trial} ← x_k + s_k

    3. EVALUATE TRIAL POINT:
       f_{k+1}^{trial} ← f(x_{k+1}^{trial})
       g_{k+1}^{trial} ← ∇f(x_{k+1}^{trial})

    4. CHECK INEXACTNESS CONDITION:
       [Verify directional inexactness bound is adequate]
       condition_met ← check_inexactness(g_k, g_{k+1}^{trial}, s_k, δ_k, M)

       WHILE NOT condition_met DO
         δ_k ← δ_k × γ_inc    [increase inexactness tolerance]

         [Re-solve subproblem with larger δ_k]
         s_k ← argmin_s { g_k^T s + ½ s^T B_k s + (M/6)||s||³ + (δ_k/2)||s||² }
         x_{k+1}^{trial} ← x_k + s_k
         f_{k+1}^{trial} ← f(x_{k+1}^{trial})
         g_{k+1}^{trial} ← ∇f(x_{k+1}^{trial})

         condition_met ← check_inexactness(g_k, g_{k+1}^{trial}, s_k, δ_k, M)
       END WHILE

    5. COMPUTE RATIO:
       pred ← -g_k^T s_k - ½ s_k^T B_k s_k - (M/6)||s_k||³
       ρ_k ← (f_k - f_{k+1}^{trial}) / pred

    6. ACCEPT/REJECT AND UPDATE σ:
       IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
         x_{k+1} ← x_{k+1}^{trial}
         g_{k+1} ← g_{k+1}^{trial}
         [Update σ via Algorithm 2a/2b]
       ELSE
         x_{k+1} ← x_k
         g_{k+1} ← g_k
         σ_{k+1} ← γ × σ_k    [increase regularization]
       END IF

    7. UPDATE HESSIAN APPROXIMATION:
       IF step was accepted THEN
         y_k ← g_{k+1} - g_k
         [Apply QN update: Algorithm 4a]
         (B_{k+1}, update_status) ← update_qn(B_k, s_k, y_k, qn_method, qn_memory)

         [Track SR1 skips for refresh trigger]
         IF update_status = "skipped" THEN
           consecutive_skips ← consecutive_skips + 1
         ELSE
           consecutive_skips ← 0
         END IF
       ELSE
         B_{k+1} ← B_k
       END IF

    8. CHECK REFRESH TRIGGERS (Hybrid Mode):
       IF refresh_available THEN
         needs_refresh ← FALSE

         [Trigger 1: Inexactness tolerance degraded]
         IF δ_k > δ_max THEN
           needs_refresh ← TRUE
           reason ← "δ_k exceeded threshold"
         END IF

         [Trigger 2: SR1 has skipped too many consecutive updates]
         IF consecutive_skips ≥ 5 THEN
           needs_refresh ← TRUE
           reason ← "SR1 skip threshold reached"
         END IF

         [Trigger 3: Periodic refresh (optional)]
         IF refresh_period > 0 AND (k mod refresh_period) = 0 THEN
           needs_refresh ← TRUE
           reason ← "periodic refresh"
         END IF

         [Trigger 4: Stagnation detected (from Algorithm 6a)]
         IF stagnation_detected THEN
           needs_refresh ← TRUE
           reason ← "stagnation"
         END IF

         IF needs_refresh THEN
           [Refresh with exact Hessian]
           B_{k+1} ← hess(x_{k+1})
           δ_{k+1} ← δ_0              [Reset inexactness tolerance]
           consecutive_skips ← 0       [Clear skip counter]
           [Clear QN history for limited-memory methods]
           IF qn_method in {"lbfgs", "lsr1"} THEN
             clear history
           END IF
           [Log refresh event for diagnostics]
           IF trace ≥ 2 THEN
             log("Hessian refresh at iteration", k, "reason:", reason)
           END IF
         END IF
       END IF

    9. UPDATE INEXACTNESS FOR NEXT ITERATION:
       IF NOT needs_refresh THEN
         δ_{k+1} ← δ_k    [carry forward; may be adjusted in step 4]
       END IF
       [Otherwise δ_{k+1} was already reset to δ_0 in step 8]

    k ← k + 1
  END WHILE

  RETURN (x_k, B_k)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUBROUTINE: check_inexactness(g_k, g_{k+1}, s_k, δ_k, M)
  [Check stopping condition from Kamzolov et al. Theorem 2.2]

  grad_inner ← g_{k+1}^T (x_{k+1}^{trial} - x_k)    [= g_{k+1}^T s_k]
  grad_norm ← ||g_{k+1}||
  step_norm ← ||s_k||

  threshold1 ← grad_norm² / (4 × δ_k)
  threshold2 ← grad_norm^{3/2} / sqrt(3 × M)

  RETURN grad_inner ≤ min(threshold1, threshold2)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

NOTES:

1. The extra δ_k/2 ||s||² term in the subproblem accounts for Hessian
   inexactness. When δ_k is small (accurate B_k), this term is negligible
   and we recover standard cubic regularization behavior.

2. The adaptive δ_k framework provides real-time control:
   - When B_k is accurate: δ_k stays small → fast CRN-like convergence
   - When B_k is poor: δ_k grows → graceful degradation to GD-like rates

3. Convergence rates (Kamzolov et al. Theorem 2.2):
   - Non-accelerated: O(max(δ_T R²/ε, sqrt(M R³/ε))) iterations
   - When δ_k controlled: O(sqrt(M R³/ε)) matching full CRN

4. The check_inexactness subroutine implements the stopping condition
   that ensures the inexact subproblem solution is adequate.

5. HYBRID MODE (when exact Hessian available):
   - Initialization: B_0 = H(x_0) provides much better starting point than γI
   - Refresh triggers prevent prolonged poor approximation periods:
     * δ_k > δ_max: Inexactness tolerance degraded beyond threshold
     * SR1 skips ≥ 5: Update formula rejected too many times consecutively
     * Stagnation: Optimizer stuck (detected by Algorithm 6a)
     * Periodic: Optional forced refresh every N iterations
   - Cost-benefit: One exact H(x_k) evaluation prevents many wasted QN iterations
   - After refresh: Reset δ_k = δ_0 and clear L-BFGS/L-SR1 history

6. Pure QN mode (no Hessian function):
   - B_0 = fd_hessian_from_grad(∇f, x_0), i.e. a one-time FD Hessian
     built from the supplied gradient (cost: 2n gradient evaluations
     at startup). This is the default as of v0.1.1. Identity or
     Barzilai-Borwein initialization is available only via an
     explicit hess = function(x) diag(length(x)) opt-out.
   - FD refresh available: B_k can be rebuilt from a fresh FD Hessian
     at the current iterate when the state-aware hybrid router detects
     drift or stall (see Algorithm 4a-hybrid).
   - Relies on the cubic subproblem's natural robustness to indefinite
     approximations and on δ_k adaptation for inexact-gradient
     problems.
```

## Algorithm 4a: Quasi-Newton Hessian Updates

Specifies BFGS and SR1 update formulas for maintaining the Hessian approximation.

```
ALGORITHM 4a: Quasi-Newton Hessian Updates
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  B_k           Current Hessian approximation (n × n)
  s_k           Step vector: s_k = x_{k+1} - x_k
  y_k           Gradient difference: y_k = g_{k+1} - g_k
  method        "bfgs", "sr1", "lbfgs", or "lsr1"
  m             Memory parameter for limited-memory variants
  skip_tol      SR1 skip threshold (default: 1e-8)
  history       List of (s_j, y_j) pairs for limited-memory

OUTPUT:
  B_{k+1}       Updated Hessian approximation
  history       Updated history (for limited-memory)

PROCEDURE:

  CASE method = "bfgs":
    [BFGS rank-2 update - maintains positive definiteness]

    ρ_bfgs ← 1 / (y_k^T s_k)

    IF y_k^T s_k > machine_epsilon THEN
      [Standard BFGS formula]
      B_{k+1} ← B_k - (B_k s_k s_k^T B_k)/(s_k^T B_k s_k) + ρ_bfgs × y_k y_k^T
    ELSE
      [Curvature condition violated - skip update]
      B_{k+1} ← B_k
    END IF

  CASE method = "sr1":
    [SR1 rank-1 update - allows indefiniteness]

    r_k ← y_k - B_k s_k
    denom ← r_k^T s_k

    [Skip test: avoid numerical instability]
    IF |denom| ≥ skip_tol × ||r_k|| × ||s_k|| THEN
      B_{k+1} ← B_k + (r_k r_k^T) / denom
    ELSE
      [Denominator too small - skip update]
      B_{k+1} ← B_k
      increment skip_counter

      [Restart if too many consecutive skips]
      IF skip_counter > restart_threshold THEN
        B_{k+1} ← (y_k^T s_k / s_k^T s_k) × I    [Barzilai-Borwein scaling]
        reset skip_counter
      END IF
    END IF

  CASE method = "lbfgs":
    [Limited-memory BFGS with m stored pairs]

    [Add new pair to history]
    history ← append(history, (s_k, y_k))
    IF length(history) > m THEN
      history ← drop_oldest(history)
    END IF

    [B_{k+1} represented implicitly via two-loop recursion]
    B_{k+1} ← (γ_k I, history)    [compact representation]

    [Scaling: γ_k = y_k^T s_k / y_k^T y_k]
    γ_k ← (y_k^T s_k) / (y_k^T y_k)

  CASE method = "lsr1":
    [Limited-memory SR1 with m stored pairs]

    r_k ← y_k - B_k s_k    [B_k applied via compact form]
    denom ← r_k^T s_k

    IF |denom| ≥ skip_tol × ||r_k|| × ||s_k|| THEN
      history ← append(history, (s_k, y_k))
      IF length(history) > m THEN
        history ← drop_oldest(history)
      END IF
    END IF

    B_{k+1} ← (γ_k I, history)    [compact representation]

  RETURN (B_{k+1}, history)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NOTES:

1. BFGS maintains B_k ≻ 0 by construction when y_k^T s_k > 0 (curvature
   condition). This wastes ARC's ability to handle indefinite Hessians.

2. SR1 can represent indefinite B_k, which pairs naturally with ARC's
   cubic regularization. Prefer SR1 for nonconvex problems.

3. Limited-memory variants store O(mn) instead of O(n²). The Hessian-
   vector product B_k v is computed via two-loop recursion in O(mn).

4. For limited-memory methods, the compact representation is:
   B_m = γ_k I + W^T C W
   where W contains s_j and y_j vectors, enabling Algorithm 5b.
```

## Algorithm 4a-hybrid: Hybrid BFGS/SR1 Update with Powell Damping

Smart routing between BFGS and SR1 based on curvature conditions, with
Powell damping as a guaranteed fallback. Two variants are defined, differing
only in update-order priority:

- **BFGS-first** (shown here): used when the state-aware router (see
  Algorithm 4a-router) is in `"pd"` mode -- B_k is reliably positive
  definite and recent predictions have tracked the true objective. BFGS
  is tried first for its Q-superlinear local rate, with SR1 and Powell
  damping as fallbacks.
- **SR1-first**: used when the router is in `"indefinite"` mode --
  B_k has negative eigenvalues or predictions have been drifting. SR1
  is tried first so indefinite curvature information is preserved
  across iterations (BFGS's rank-2 update would destroy it). Fallbacks
  are BFGS and Powell damping, in that order.

```
ALGORITHM 4a-hybrid: Hybrid BFGS/SR1 Update with Powell Damping
                     (BFGS-first variant; used in "pd" routing mode)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  B_k           Current Hessian approximation (n × n)
  s_k           Step vector: s_k = x_{k+1} - x_k
  y_k           Gradient difference: y_k = g_{k+1} - g_k
  bfgs_tol      BFGS curvature tolerance (default: 1e-10)
  sr1_skip_tol  SR1 skip threshold (default: 1e-8)
  skip_count    Current consecutive SR1 skip count
  restart_threshold  Skips before restart (default: 5)

OUTPUT:
  B_{k+1}       Updated Hessian approximation
  update_type   "bfgs" | "sr1" | "powell" | "skipped"
  skip_count    Updated skip counter

PROCEDURE:

  1. TRY BFGS (stable, maintains positive definiteness):
     ys ← y_k^T s_k

     IF ys > bfgs_tol THEN
       B_{k+1} ← B_k - (B_k s_k s_k^T B_k)/(s_k^T B_k s_k) + (y_k y_k^T)/ys
       RETURN (B_{k+1}, "bfgs", 0)
     END IF

  2. FALL BACK TO SR1 (allows indefiniteness):
     r_k ← y_k - B_k s_k
     denom ← r_k^T s_k

     IF |denom| ≥ sr1_skip_tol × ||r_k|| × ||s_k|| THEN
       B_{k+1} ← B_k + (r_k r_k^T) / denom
       RETURN (B_{k+1}, "sr1", 0)
     END IF

  3. FALL BACK TO POWELL-DAMPED BFGS (guaranteed update):
     bs ← B_k s_k
     sbs ← s_k^T bs

     [Powell damping: modify y to ensure curvature condition]
     IF ys < 0.2 × sbs THEN
       θ ← 0.8 × sbs / (sbs - ys)
     ELSE
       θ ← 1.0
     END IF

     y_damped ← θ × y_k + (1 - θ) × bs
     ys_damped ← y_damped^T s_k    [guaranteed ≥ 0.2 × sbs]

     B_{k+1} ← B_k - (bs bs^T)/sbs + (y_damped y_damped^T)/ys_damped
     RETURN (B_{k+1}, "powell", 0)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NOTES:

1. The routing priority is: BFGS > SR1 > Powell-damped BFGS
   - BFGS is preferred when curvature is positive (stable, well-conditioned)
   - SR1 captures negative curvature when BFGS fails
   - Powell damping guarantees an update when both fail

2. Powell damping formula ensures s^T y_damped ≥ 0.2 × s^T B s > 0.
   The 0.2 threshold provides sufficient curvature margin.

3. The θ formula comes from Powell (1978): "A fast algorithm for
   nonlinearly constrained optimization calculations."

4. This hybrid approach is especially valuable for problems that
   transition between convex and nonconvex regions during optimization.

5. Unlike pure SR1, the hybrid method rarely skips updates since
   Powell damping provides a guaranteed fallback.

6. The SR1-first variant (used in "indefinite" routing mode) simply
   swaps steps 1 and 2 above. The skip and fallback conditions are
   identical.
```

## Algorithm 4a-router: State-Aware Hybrid Routing

Tracks a routing mode ("indefinite" or "pd") that determines which
update-order variant of Algorithm 4a-hybrid to apply on each step.
Also triggers FD Hessian refreshes when the iteration appears stuck.

```
ALGORITHM 4a-router: State-Aware Hybrid Routing
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE (persists across iterations):
  routing_mode         "indefinite" (initial) or "pd"
  bad_rho_count        consecutive rho < demote_rho steps (in "pd" mode)
  pd_count             consecutive good PD steps (in "indefinite" mode)
  indef_bad_rho_count  consecutive rho < demote_rho (in "indefinite" mode)
  indef_iter_count     iterations spent in "indefinite" mode

INPUT (per iteration):
  B_k                  current Hessian approximation
  rho_k                cubic-subproblem ratio from most recent step
  demote_rho           default 0.25
  promote_rho          default 0.5
  demote_k             default 2
  promote_k            default 3
  fd_refresh_k         default 3  (drift refresh)
  stuck_refresh_k      default 100 (stall refresh)

PROCEDURE (after each accepted step, before next iteration):

  1. UPDATE STATE COUNTERS:
     b_is_pd ← (chol(B_k) succeeds)
     IF NOT b_is_pd THEN
       routing_mode ← "indefinite"    [immediate demotion]
     END IF

     IF rho_k < demote_rho THEN
       bad_rho_count ← bad_rho_count + 1
     ELSE
       bad_rho_count ← 0
     END IF
     IF b_is_pd AND rho_k > promote_rho THEN
       pd_count ← pd_count + 1
     ELSE
       pd_count ← 0
     END IF

  2. MODE TRANSITIONS:
     IF routing_mode = "pd" AND bad_rho_count ≥ demote_k THEN
       routing_mode ← "indefinite"    [prediction drift]
       bad_rho_count ← 0
     ELSE IF routing_mode = "indefinite" AND pd_count ≥ promote_k THEN
       routing_mode ← "pd"            [B reliably PD, rho consistent]
       pd_count ← 0
     END IF

  3. FD REFRESH TRIGGERS (only while in "indefinite" mode):
     indef_iter_count ← indef_iter_count + 1
     IF rho_k < demote_rho THEN
       indef_bad_rho_count ← indef_bad_rho_count + 1
     ELSE
       indef_bad_rho_count ← 0
     END IF

     drift   ← (indef_bad_rho_count ≥ fd_refresh_k)
     stall   ← (indef_iter_count ≥ stuck_refresh_k)

     IF drift OR stall THEN
       B_k ← fd_hessian_from_grad(∇f, x_k)    [2n gradient evals]
       RESET all state counters
     END IF

OUTPUT: (routing_mode, possibly-refreshed B_k)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NOTES:

1. The two refresh triggers are complementary:
   - Drift: rho is low per-step → current B_k makes poor predictions
     → rebuild from truth.
   - Stall: B_k has stayed indefinite for a long time with locally
     acceptable rho → iteration is tracking a secondary asymmetric
     saddle → rebuild to escape.

2. Empirical: on a growth-mixture-model symmetric saddle (6 params,
   50 random datasets), combining the state-aware routing with both
   refresh triggers raises the local-min recovery rate of hybrid QN
   from ~12% (identity init, BFGS-first only) to 100%, matching
   full-Hessian solvers.

3. The thresholds (0.25, 0.5, 2, 3, 3, 100) are standard trust-region
   ratios with hysteresis and are exposed as control parameters
   (qn_route_*, qn_fd_refresh_k, qn_stuck_refresh_k).
```

## Algorithm 4a-lhybrid: Limited-Memory Hybrid BFGS/SR1 Update

Limited-memory version of Algorithm 4a-hybrid. Same routing logic but operates on
history vectors instead of explicit Hessian matrix.

```
ALGORITHM 4a-lhybrid: Limited-Memory Hybrid BFGS/SR1 with Powell Damping
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  H_k           History structure: {s[], y[], rho[], gamma}
  s_k           Step vector: s_k = x_{k+1} - x_k
  y_k           Gradient difference: y_k = g_{k+1} - g_k
  m             Memory size (default: 10)
  bfgs_tol      BFGS curvature tolerance (default: 1e-10)
  sr1_skip_tol  SR1 skip threshold (default: 1e-8)

OUTPUT:
  H_{k+1}       Updated history structure
  update_type   "lbfgs" | "lsr1" | "powell"
  theta         Damping parameter (1.0 if no damping)

PROCEDURE:

  1. TRY L-BFGS (stable, maintains positive definiteness):
     ys ← y_k^T s_k

     IF ys > bfgs_tol THEN
       H_{k+1} ← UPDATE_LBFGS(H_k, s_k, y_k, m)
       RETURN (H_{k+1}, "lbfgs", 1.0)
     END IF

  2. FALL BACK TO L-SR1 (allows indefiniteness):
     [Compute B_k s_k via compact form or lsr1_multiply]
     γ ← H_k.gamma (or 1.0 if H_k is NULL)
     b_s ← γ × s_k + Σ_i (correction terms from history)

     r_k ← y_k - b_s
     denom ← r_k^T s_k

     IF |denom| ≥ sr1_skip_tol × ||r_k|| × ||s_k|| THEN
       H_{k+1} ← UPDATE_LSR1(H_k, s_k, y_k, m, b_times_s = b_s)
       RETURN (H_{k+1}, "lsr1", 1.0)
     END IF

  3. FALL BACK TO POWELL-DAMPED L-BFGS (guaranteed update):
     sbs ← s_k^T b_s

     [Powell damping: modify y to ensure curvature condition]
     IF ys < 0.2 × sbs THEN
       θ ← 0.8 × sbs / (sbs - ys)
     ELSE
       θ ← 1.0
     END IF

     y_damped ← θ × y_k + (1 - θ) × b_s
     H_{k+1} ← UPDATE_LBFGS(H_k, s_k, y_damped, m)
     RETURN (H_{k+1}, "powell", θ)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NOTES:

1. Computing B_k s_k in limited-memory:
   - L-SR1: Use lsr1_multiply(H_k, s_k) directly
   - L-BFGS: Use compact form B = γI + W^T C W, then b_s = γs + W^T(C(Ws))

2. When H_k is NULL (first iteration), B_k = γI, so b_s = γ × s_k.

3. The damped y is stored in L-BFGS history, ensuring all future
   implicit Hessian-vector products use the damped curvature.

4. Memory complexity: O(mn) for m stored pairs, same as standard L-BFGS.
```

## Algorithm 4b: Accelerated Inexact Cubic QN

Nesterov-style acceleration achieving $O(k^{-2})$ convergence rate.

```
ALGORITHM 4b: Accelerated Inexact Cubic Regularized QN
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_0           Initial point
  B_0           Initial Hessian approximation
  A_0 = 0       Initial acceleration parameter
  [All parameters from Algorithm 4]

OUTPUT:
  x*            Approximate minimizer

PROCEDURE:
  k ← 0
  v_0 ← x_0     [auxiliary sequence]

  WHILE NOT converged DO

    1. COMPUTE ACCELERATION PARAMETERS:
       [Solve for a_{k+1}: a_{k+1}² = A_k + a_{k+1}]
       a_{k+1} ← (1 + sqrt(1 + 4 A_k)) / 2
       A_{k+1} ← A_k + a_{k+1}

       [Interpolation point]
       τ_k ← a_{k+1} / A_{k+1}
       y_k ← τ_k × v_k + (1 - τ_k) × x_k

    2. EVALUATE AT INTERPOLATION POINT:
       f(y_k), g(y_k), B(y_k)

    3. SOLVE ACCELERATED CUBIC SUBPROBLEM:
       [Same as Algorithm 4, Step 2, but at y_k]
       s_k ← solve_inexact_cubic(g(y_k), B(y_k), M, δ_k)
       x_{k+1}^{trial} ← y_k + s_k

    4. ADAPTIVE INEXACTNESS CHECK:
       [Same as Algorithm 4, Step 4]

    5. COMPUTE RATIO AND ACCEPT/REJECT:
       [Same as Algorithm 4, Steps 5-6]

       IF step accepted THEN
         x_{k+1} ← x_{k+1}^{trial}

         [Update auxiliary sequence]
         v_{k+1} ← v_k - a_{k+1} × ∇f(x_{k+1}) / (some scaling)
       ELSE
         x_{k+1} ← x_k
         v_{k+1} ← v_k
       END IF

    6. UPDATE HESSIAN APPROXIMATION:
       [Same as Algorithm 4, Step 7]

    k ← k + 1
  END WHILE

  RETURN x_k

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NOTES:

1. Convergence rate: O(max(sqrt(δ R² / ε), (M R³ / ε)^{1/3})) iterations
   - With history-based QN: O(sqrt(L_1 R² / ε)) matching AGM
   - With HVP sampling: O((M R³ / ε)^{1/3}) matching accelerated CRN

2. The auxiliary sequence v_k provides the extrapolation that enables
   acceleration. When δ_k is well-controlled, this achieves optimal
   rates for convex functions.

3. For nonconvex problems, use non-accelerated Algorithm 4 instead.
   Acceleration is designed for convex optimization.
```

**Quasi-Newton Parameters**:

| Parameter | Default | Valid Range | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `use_qn` | FALSE | — | Enable quasi-Newton mode |
| `qn_method` | "sr1" | "bfgs", "sr1", "lbfgs", "lsr1" | QN update formula |
| `qn_memory` | 10 | $[3, 50]$ | History pairs for L-BFGS/L-SR1 |
| `delta_init` | 1.0 | $(0, \infty)$ | Initial inexactness tolerance |
| `delta_inc` | 2.0 | $(1, \infty)$ | Inexactness increase factor |
| `sr1_skip_tol` | $10^{-8}$ | $(0, 1)$ | SR1 denominator threshold |
| `sr1_restart_after` | 5 | $[1, \infty)$ | Skips before restart |
| `use_accel` | FALSE | — | Enable Nesterov acceleration (convex only) |

# Cubic Subproblem Solver

The cubic subproblem $\min_s m_k(s)$ is the computational core of ARC. arcopt provides two solver options optimized for different problem scales and structures.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd §sec-cubic-subproblem for detailed comparison, hard-case discussion, and theoretical background.*

## Overview: Two Solver Approaches

arcopt offers two cubic subproblem solvers that users can select via `control$cubic_solver`:

| Solver | Best For | Complexity | Key Advantage |
|--------|----------|------------|---------------|
| **"eigen"** (5a) | Dense H, n ≤ 500 | O(n³) | Exact eigenvalues, clean hard-case handling |
| **"cg"** (5b) | Sparse H, n > 500, or Hv products only | O(n·J·m) | Scalable, matrix-free, automatic negative curvature detection |

**Default selection** (when `cubic_solver = "auto"`):
- If n ≤ 500 and full Hessian available: use **eigendecomposition (5a)**
- If n > 500 or only Hessian-vector products available: use **ARCqK (5b)**

### The Secular Equation

Both solvers work by finding the optimal regularization multiplier $\lambda$ that satisfies:

$$\phi(\lambda) = \|s(\lambda)\| - \frac{\lambda}{\sigma} = 0$$

where $s(\lambda) = -(H + \lambda I)^{-1}g$ is the regularized Newton step.

**Key properties:**
- $\phi(\lambda) \to +\infty$ as $\lambda \to -\lambda_{\min}^+$ (smallest eigenvalue of H)
- $\phi(\lambda) \to -\infty$ as $\lambda \to +\infty$
- $\phi$ is strictly decreasing for $\lambda > -\lambda_{\min}$
- Unique root exists in $(\max(0, -\lambda_{\min}), +\infty)$ in the **easy case**

**Hard case exception:** When $\lambda_{\min}(H) < 0$ AND $g \perp v_{\min}$ (gradient orthogonal to the smallest eigenvector), there is no root in the valid interval. The optimal solution requires setting $\lambda^* = -\lambda_{\min}$ and adding a component along $v_{\min}$ to reach the target norm.

## Algorithm 5a: Eigendecomposition-Based Cubic Solver

This approach computes the full eigendecomposition $H = Q \Lambda Q^T$ and solves the secular equation in spectral coordinates. It provides exact eigenvalues for hard-case detection and works cleanly with indefinite Hessians.

**When to use:** Small-to-medium dense problems (n ≤ 500), analytic or finite-difference Hessians, or when exact eigenvalue information is desired for diagnostics.

```
ALGORITHM 5a: Eigendecomposition-Based Cubic Solver
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  g           Gradient vector (length n)
  H           Hessian matrix (n × n, may be indefinite)
  σ           Regularization parameter (> 0)
  tol_lambda  Secular equation tolerance (e.g., 1e-10)
  tol_hard    Hard-case detection threshold (e.g., 1e-8)
  max_iter    Maximum secular iterations (e.g., 50)

OUTPUT:
  s           Solution to cubic subproblem
  λ           Final regularization multiplier
  converged   TRUE if secular equation converged
  case_type   "easy", "hard", or "zero_gradient"

PROCEDURE:
  0. HANDLE ZERO GRADIENT:
     IF ||g|| < machine_epsilon THEN
       RETURN (s = 0, λ = 0, converged = TRUE, case_type = "zero_gradient")
     END IF

  1. EIGENDECOMPOSITION:
     (Λ, Q) ← eigen(H)    [Λ = diag(λ₁, ..., λₙ), λ₁ ≤ ... ≤ λₙ]
     g̃ ← Q^T g           [transform gradient to spectral coordinates]

  2. DETERMINE LOWER BOUND:
     λ_lower ← max(0, -λ₁)
     [If λ₁ < 0, need shift ≥ -λ₁ to ensure H + λI ≻ 0]

  3. CHECK FOR HARD CASE:
     IF λ₁ < 0 AND |g̃₁| < tol_hard × ||g|| THEN
       [HARD CASE: g nearly orthogonal to smallest eigenvector]
       λ* ← -λ₁
       s̃ ← zeros(n)

       [Compute s̃ᵢ = -g̃ᵢ/(λᵢ + λ*) for i where λᵢ + λ* is not too small]
       FOR i = 2 TO n DO
         IF |λᵢ + λ*| > machine_epsilon THEN
           s̃[i] ← -g̃[i] / (λᵢ + λ*)
         ELSE
           s̃[i] ← 0
         END IF
       END FOR

       [Add component along v₁ to satisfy secular equation]
       s_partial_norm ← ||s̃[2:n]||
       target_norm ← λ* / σ
       IF target_norm² > s_partial_norm² THEN
         τ ← sqrt(target_norm² - s_partial_norm²)
         s̃[1] ← τ    [or -τ, either direction works]
       ELSE
         s̃[1] ← 0
       END IF

       s ← Q s̃    [transform back to original coordinates]
       RETURN (s, λ*, converged = TRUE, case_type = "hard")
     END IF

  4. EASY CASE: SECULAR EQUATION ITERATION
     [Initialize λ > λ_lower to ensure positive definiteness]
     λ ← λ_lower + 0.1 × max(1, |λ_lower|)
     converged ← FALSE

     FOR iter = 1 TO max_iter DO
       [Compute step in spectral coordinates: s̃ᵢ = -g̃ᵢ/(λᵢ + λ)]
       FOR i = 1 TO n DO
         IF |λᵢ + λ| > machine_epsilon THEN
           s̃[i] ← -g̃[i] / (λᵢ + λ)
         ELSE
           s̃[i] ← 0    [avoid division by near-zero]
         END IF
       END FOR

       s_norm ← ||s̃||

       [Check for numerical issues]
       IF NOT is_finite(s_norm) OR s_norm < machine_epsilon THEN
         BREAK    [numerical breakdown, use current s̃]
       END IF

       [Secular equation residual]
       φ ← s_norm - λ/σ

       IF NOT is_finite(φ) THEN
         BREAK    [numerical instability]
       END IF

       [Check convergence]
       IF |φ| < tol_lambda × max(1, λ/σ) THEN
         converged ← TRUE
         BREAK
       END IF

       [Newton step on φ(λ)]
       [φ'(λ) = -Σᵢ s̃ᵢ²/(λᵢ + λ) / s_norm - 1/σ]
       sum_term ← 0
       FOR i = 1 TO n DO
         sum_term ← sum_term + s̃[i]² / (λᵢ + λ)
       END FOR
       φ_prime ← -sum_term / s_norm - 1/σ

       IF NOT is_finite(φ_prime) THEN
         BREAK    [numerical instability]
       END IF

       λ_new ← λ - φ / φ_prime
       λ ← max(λ_new, λ_lower + machine_epsilon)
     END FOR

     s ← Q s̃    [transform back to original coordinates]

  5. COMPUTE PREDICTED REDUCTION:
     pred_reduction ← -g^T s - ½ s^T H s - (σ/3) ||s||³

  RETURN (s, λ, pred_reduction, converged, case_type = "easy")

NOTES:
  - Eigendecomposition is O(n³) via LAPACK
  - Secular iteration typically converges in 3-5 iterations
  - Hard case is rare in practice but must be handled correctly
  - Sign choice for τ doesn't matter (both directions yield valid solutions)
  - For diagnostics: return eigenvalue spectrum in trace mode
```

**Implementation Status:**
- ✅ Fully implemented in `solve_cubic_eigen()`
- Used for all problem sizes by default
- See `design/historical/ldl-solver.qmd` for removed LDL approach

## Algorithm 5c: Eigendecomposition-Based Trust-Region Solver

Used as the subproblem solver after the flat-ridge detector (Algorithm 6c) triggers a one-way switch from cubic regularization. Mirrors Algorithm 5a's eigendecomposition machinery; the only structural difference is the constraint (`‖s‖ ≤ r` hard step-norm instead of `λ = σ‖s‖` via a cubic penalty).

**When to use:** After cubic-to-trust-region switch on a flat-ridge stagnation. See also design-principles §3a for the σ ↔ r duality framing.

```
ALGORITHM 5c: Trust-Region Subproblem via Eigendecomposition
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  g           Gradient vector (length n)
  H           Hessian matrix (n × n, may be indefinite)
  r           Trust-region radius (> 0)
  tol_lambda  Secular equation tolerance (e.g., 1e-10)
  tol_hard    Hard-case detection threshold (e.g., 1e-8)
  max_iter    Maximum secular iterations (e.g., 50)

OUTPUT:
  s             Solution to trust-region subproblem
  λ             Lagrange multiplier (0 if interior Newton step)
  pred_reduct   Predicted reduction −g'·s − (1/2) s'·H·s
  converged     TRUE if solver returned a valid step
  case_type     "interior", "easy", "hard", or "zero_gradient"
  on_boundary   TRUE if ‖s‖ == r (active constraint)

PROCEDURE:
  0. HANDLE ZERO GRADIENT:
     IF ||g|| < machine_epsilon THEN
       RETURN (s = 0, interior; converged = TRUE)
     END IF

  1. EIGENDECOMPOSITION:
     (Λ, Q) ← eigen(H); λ₁ ≤ λ₂ ≤ ... ≤ λₙ
     g̃ ← Q' g

  2. INTERIOR CASE (unconstrained Newton fits):
     IF λ₁ > 0 THEN
       s̃_N ← −g̃ / Λ     (componentwise)
       IF ‖s̃_N‖ ≤ r THEN
         s ← Q s̃_N
         λ ← 0; on_boundary ← FALSE
         RETURN (case_type = "interior")
       END IF
     END IF

  3. HARD CASE (Moré–Sorensen):
     IF λ₁ ≤ 0 AND |g̃₁| < tol_hard · ‖g‖ THEN
       λ* ← −λ₁
       s̃_i ← −g̃_i / (λ_i + λ*) for i ≥ 2; s̃_1 ← 0
       IF ‖s̃_{i≥2}‖ ≤ r THEN
         τ ← sqrt(r² − ‖s̃_{i≥2}‖²)
         s̃_1 ← τ          (sign arbitrary — v_1 is a null direction for Hs)
         s ← Q s̃
         λ ← λ*; on_boundary ← TRUE
         RETURN (case_type = "hard")
       END IF
       (fall through to easy case if hard-hard bound is violated)
     END IF

  4. EASY BOUNDARY CASE (secular equation):
     λ ← λ_lower + 0.1 · max(1, |λ_lower| + 1), where λ_lower = max(0, −λ₁)
     FOR iter = 1 TO max_iter DO
       s̃_i ← −g̃_i / (λ_i + λ); compute ‖s̃‖
       φ(λ) ← ‖s̃‖ − r
       IF |φ(λ)| < tol_lambda · max(1, r) THEN converged ← TRUE; BREAK
       φ'(λ) ← −(1/‖s̃‖) · Σ_i s̃_i² / (λ_i + λ)
       λ ← max(λ − φ/φ', λ_lower + ε)
     END FOR
     s ← Q s̃
     on_boundary ← TRUE
     RETURN (case_type = "easy")
```

**Implementation Status:**
- ✅ Fully implemented in `solve_tr_eigen()` (R/tr_eigen.R)
- Invoked in place of `solve_cubic_eigen()` once the flat-ridge detector (Algorithm 6c) fires
- See todo/tr-fallback-hybrid-briefing.md for design context

## Algorithm 6c: Flat-Ridge Detector

Emits a one-way switch from cubic to trust-region subproblem when four runtime signals jointly indicate that the cubic penalty is no longer providing useful damping and the iterate is stalled on a near-singular positive-definite ridge.

**Rationale**: the local error bound (EB) condition of Yue, Zhou & So (2018) is sufficient for cubic regularization to converge Q-quadratically at degenerate minimizers. A flat ridge is a regime where the EB constant is practically unbounded — no convergence guarantee applies. The four-signal rule is an empirical proxy for EB violation.

```
ALGORITHM 6c: Flat-Ridge Detector
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STATE (sliding window of length W):
  sigma[1..W]       σ values in the window
  rho[1..W]         ρ values in the window
  g_inf[1..W]       ||g||_inf values in the window
  lambda_min[1..W]  smallest Hessian eigenvalue in the window

CONTROL:
  sigma_min                 regularization floor used by the orchestrator
  tol_ridge                 λ_min upper bound for "near-singular PD"
  rho_tol                   tolerance on |ρ − 1|
  grad_decrease_max         ratio latest/oldest ||g||_inf above which
                            the gradient counts as stagnant
```
